text: DEBRA-LIBANOS, a town of Abyssinia, in the united prov. of Shoa and Efat, 30 m. NW of Tegulet. It is noted for its monastery.
DEBRA-MARIAM, a town of Abyssinia, in the kingdom of Amhara, prov. of Begemder, 70 m. SE of Gondar.
DEBRA-MULTAI, a town of Abyssinia, in the prov. of Tigre, district of Seraxo, 70 m. N of Antalo.
DEBRA-SELALO, a town of Abyssinia, in the kingdom of Amhara, prov. of Gojam, 75 m. S of Gondar, on an affluent of the Blue Nile.
DEBRE', a village of Anatolia, in the sanjak of Hamid, 18 m. SSE of Aglason.

DEBRECZIN, or Debretzin, [Hung. Debreezen.] a royal free town of Hungary, the cap. of the com. of Bihar, and the principal commercial town in Upper or Eastern Hungary, situated in N lat. 47° 32', E long. 21° 36', in the centre of an "unvaried and apparently unlimited plain" [Bright], 125 m. E of Pest, 33 m. NNW of Gross-Wardein, and 68 m. S by E of Kaschau, at an alt. of 360 ft. above sea-level. It is an open town, and composed almost entirely of thatched cottages, one story in height, built on no regular plan, and generally having their gable-ends towards the wide unpaved streets. "In summer," says the graf Bathyany, describing D. 40 years ago, "in summer you must wade through sand, and at other seasons through deep mud, even when you keep to the streets and public paths of D." And this state of matters, it appears, has undergone little improvement since the graf wrote; for Mr. Paget, who visited D. in 1835, says: "In rainy weather, the whole street becomes one liquid mass of mud, so that officers quartered on one side the street are obliged to mount their horses and ride across to dinner on the other." There are, however, several handsome buildings in D., among which are the town-hall, the Reformed church and college, several Protestant and Roman Catholic churches, and a Piarist monastery. It is also the seat of the Distriktual-tafel, or court-ofappeal, for the prov. on the E side of the Theiss. The college was founded in 1792, and possesses a library of 20,000 vols. The number of students attending this college in 1814 was 550 togati; but Paget states that the whole number of togati and nonparallel of 15° 45' N, about 200 m. W by S of Tim- togati at D. is upwards of 2,000. The togati, so called from a peculiar black gown or toga which they wear, are such as intend to dedicate themselves to the church or to teaching; the non-togati embrace all others. The pop. in 1827 was returned at 45,375; in 1846, at 63,000. It is chiefly composed of Magyars, whose language is here spoken in its greatest purity, and whose costume, manners, and national characteristics, may be most advantageously studied here. The principal manufactures of D. are shoes, leather, soap, tobacco-pipes, and pipe-bowls, pottery, cutlery, combs, buttons, soda, and saltpetre. It is likewise the great mart for the produce of the N and E of Hungary. Fairs are held without the town every 3 months, when an extent which the eye can scarcely command is covered with flocks and waggons, bales and cases, tents and huts, amid which thousands of people are congregated. Cattle, hogs, horses, bacon, wheat, millet, tobacco, wine, flax, linen, honey, &c., are largely sold at these fairs; and here a great part of the small traders of Transylvania obtain their supplies of Vienna goods and colonial produce. A large proportion of the poorer inhabitants of D. depend for their subsistence on fields situated upon the surrounding plain, and several days' journey from their houses. To these they retire with their families and cattle for a fortnight, two or three times a-year, as the cultivation of the land or the harvest may require; living in a hut, a tent, or a waggon, according to their circumstances, and the state of the weather. Others, again, quitting the villages in which they live, go far into the plain to attend the flocks and herds of their superiors, leading, for many weeks or months without interruption, a life of hardship and privation, which custom has taught them to bear with indifference. The whole of the level tract is divided into what are called prædia, which belong to different nobles; and in this district there are villages, the property of single individuals, which contain 2,000 or 3,000 peasants, each of whom possesses an entire session of land. [Bright.] Natron, in Hungary named szek-so, occurs in great abundance in the neighbourhood of D., where it is contained in solution in the marshes and lakes which extend on all sides into the plain, but especially between D. and Nagyvarad, and on the heaths in the neighbourhood of Kis-Maria. These lakes dry up in summer, and the saline efflorescences, which at that period resemble heaps of sand, are renewed at the end of 3 or 4 days after being removed, so that during the whole season great quantities are collected, which are carried to D. as well for exportation as the manufacture of soap. [Beudant.]—D. was taken by the Turks in 1684, but abandoned by them the same year. In 1715 it was created a free town by Charles VI. The national diet issued the Hungarian declaration of independence at D. on the 14th April, 1849. The sitting, "in order that the resolutions to be taken might be attended with the publicity and solemnity befitting so great an occasion," was held in the great Reformed church; and, on the same occasion, Ludgwig Kossuth, president of the committee of national defence, was chosen by acclamation first president of the new republic.
DEBRO, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 13 m. NNW of Heves, on the r. bank of the Tarna, celebrated for the excellent tobacco which is grown in its vicinity.
DEBTLING, or DEPTLING, a parish of Kent, 23 m. NE of Maidstone. Area 1,430 acres. Pop. 318.
DEBYOSUI, or DEBESY, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Viatka, near the frontiers of Perm, and 60 m. W by S of Okhansk, at an alt. of 860 ft. above sealevel.

DECATUR, a county of the state of Georgia, U. S., comprising an area of 1,675 sq. m., bordered on | the Ghauts; but in general the hills rise above it, to DEC the W by the Chattahuchi river, and intersected by Flint and Spring rivers. The surface is undulating, but it possesses little fertility. Pop. in 1830, 3,854; in 1840, 5,872. Its cap. is Bainbridge.—Also a co. in the SE of the state of Indiana, comprising a finely undulating surface of 340 sq. m., intersected by Flat Rock, Clifty, and Sand creeks. Its soil is highly productive. Pop. in 1840, 12,171. Its cap. is Greensburgh.—Also a township of Otsego co., in the state of New York, 61 m. SW of Albany, drained by Elk creek. It is generally hilly; its soil consists of sandy loam. Pop. 1,071.—Also a township of Mifflin co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 14 m. NE of Lewisburg, watered by Jack's creek, an affluent of the Juniata. It has a mountainous surface, and a gravelly soil. Pop. 767.—Also a village of Byrd township, Brown co., in the state of Ohio, 105 m. SW of Columbus. Pop. 150.—Also a township of Van Buren co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. 328.—Also a village of De Kalb co., in the state of Georgia, 90 m. NW of Milledgeville, near the Georgia railroad. In 1840 it consisted of about 50 dwellings.—Also a township of Marion co., in the state of Indiana. Pop. 916 .-- Also a village of Adams co., in the same state, 132 m. NE of Indianapolis, on the SW side of the Wabash river.—Also a township of Washington co., in the state of Ohio. Pop. 439.—Also a township of Lawrence co., in the same state. Pop. 594.—Also a village of Morgan co., in the state of Alabama, 139 m. NNE of Tuscaloosa, on the S side of Tennessee river, and at the E extremity of the Tuscumbia and Decatur railroad. — Also a village of Newton co., in the state of Massachusetts, 96 m. E of Jackson, on the head-waters of Chickasawha river.—Also a village of Meig's co., in the state of Tennessee, 140 m. ESE of Nashville, and 21 m. E of Tennessee river.—Also a village of Macon co., in the state of Illinois, 40 m. E of Springfield, on the W side of the N fork of Sangamon river, and on the borders of an extensive prairie. In 1840 it consisted of about 35 dwellings.
DECAZEVILLE, or LA SALLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Aubun, 22 m. from Villefranche. Pop. in 1841, 4,154. It has some iron-works.

DECCAN, or DAK'HIN, i. e. 'the South,' or 'the Southern country,' a term applied by Hindu writers originally to all that portion of Hindostan which lies to the S of the Nerbudda river; but afterwards restricted to the territory lying between the Nerbudda on the N, and the Kistna or Krishna on the S, and having the Indian ocean on the W, and the gulf of Bengal on the E. The D. thus comprehends the modern divisions of Candeish, Orissa, Gundwana, Berar, the N. Circars, Bider, Aurungabad, Hyderabad, and Bejapur; to which some add the Balaghaut territory to the S of the Krishna. The term Hindostan, as distinguished from the D., is confined strictly to Gangetic India, or the country between the Himalayas and the Nerbudda. The grand geographical feature of this region is the chain of Ghauts which run along its W side for its whole length, having the Concan between it and the sea. This range extends from 40 to 50 m. in breadth; and includes many fertile spots, producing abundance of grain; but in general it is very rough, and intersected by steep and rocky hills. Towards the Ghauts the country is in most places extremely strong, divided by hills, crossed by ravines, and covered with thick forest. The range itself is from 2,000 to 4,000 ft. high, and extremely abrupt and inaccessible on the W. The passes are numerous, but steep, and seldom passable for carriages. The table-land on the E is nearly as high as many parts of the ridge of the height of from 1,000 to 1,500 ft. The table-land is for a considerable distance rendered very strong by numerous spurs issuing from the range, among which occur deep winding rugged valleys often filled with thick jungle. Further E the branches from the Ghauts become less frequent, and the country more level, till we approach the Nizam's frontier, where it is an open plain. The N part of the chain of Ghauts, and the country at its base, is inhabited by Bhils; that part to the S of Baugland and its base as far S as Bassein, is inhabited by Coolies, a tribe resembling the former, but somewhat less idle and predatory in their habits. The Bhils possess the E part of the range, and all the branches that run out from it towards the E, as far S as Punah; they are also spread over the plains to the E, especially on the N of the Godaveri, and to the neighbourhood of the Wurda. On the N they extend beyond the Tapti and Nerbudda. Both the Bhils and the Coolies are numerous in Guzerat. To the S of Punah, the Bhils are succeeded by the Ramuses, a more civilized and subdued tribe, but with the same thievish habits. The Ramuses have no language of their own, are more mixed with the people, and resemble the Mahrattas in dress and manners, whereas the Bhils differ from the rest of the people in language, manners, and appearance. Of the latter, Mr. Elphinstone remarks that, although they live quietly in the open country, they resume their wild and predatory character whenever they are settled in a part that is strong, either from hills or jungle. The Ramuses do not extend further S than Colapore, or further E than the line of Bejapur. The plain of Candeish descends towards the Tapti from the hills on the N and S. On the E it is and extensive jungle. Though interspersed with low ranges of unproductive hills the bulk of this prov. is exceedingly fertile, and it is watered by innumerable streams, on many of which expensive embankments have formerly been erected for purposes of irrigation. Some parts of the prov. are still in a high state of cultivation; others, more recently abandoned, convey a high notion of their former richness and prosperity; but the greater part is covered with jungle full of tigers and other wild beasts, but strewed with the ruins of former villages. The districts N of the Tapti in particular, which were formerly very populous and yielded a large revenue, are now almost an uninhabited forest. Candeish is low and hot. Gungterry, which joins it on the S, is from 1,500 to 2,000 ft. above the level of the Tapti; the rest of the conquered territory—excepting the Concan—is on the same table-land. From this to the Kristna, or rather to the Warna and the Kristna, is comprehended in the districts of Ahmednuggur and Punah, and the rajah of Sattarah's territory. The W half of this tract is hilly, with rich and highly cultivated valleys, and the country beautiful and diversified. Further E are plains, not all in the same condition. The E of Gunterry, though open and fertile, is almost entirely uninhabited since the famine in 1803. The country between that and Ahmednuggur is better, and the plains S of Ahmednuggur are, for many marches in all directions, one sheet of the richest cultivation. The SE portion of that dist. towards Colapore, is perhaps equally prosperous. The country beyond the Neera is in a very different state, thinly peopled and badly cultivated. It is in this tract that most of the horses in the Mahratta country are bred, and that most of the silladars, or military adventurers, reside. The principal towns, in the Peishwa's late | dominions, are between Candeish and the Kristna; but none of them are considerable. The Mahratta language and nation extend from the Satpura mountains nearly to the Kristna; and from the sea on the W, to a waving frontier on the E, which may be indicated by a line drawn from Goa to the Wurda near Chanda, and thence along that river to the Satpura range. The whole of this territory was probably under a Mahratta king who resided at Deoguri, now Dowlatabad. But this empire was finally subverted by the Mahommedans in the 14th cent. The Mahommedans invaded the D. towards the close of the 13th cent. On the extinction of the Bhamini dynasty in 1518, it was divided into 5 independent principalities, viz. Bejapur, Golconda, Ahmednuggur, Bider, and Berar. Towards the close of the 16th cent. the Moguls began to invade the D., but their conquest of it was not completed until 1700. After the death of Aurungzebe, the Mahrattas seized on the W districts; but the E remained under the government of a nizam, or viceroy, appointed by the Mogul, who ultimately became virtual sovereign of the territories committed to his care. The N. Circars were acquired by the English from the nizam in 1766, and Orissa, which had always been annexed to Bengal, had followed the fortunes of that prov. In 1818, Candeish was acquired from Mulhar Rao Holkar; and Punah, with the Concan, and the Southern Mahratta country, was wrested from the Peishwa. The territory thus acquired by the English was divided into 4 collectorates, the general statistics of which in 1827-8 were as follows: Cultivated Revenue. Pop. Area. begahs. Of the above pop. from 60 to 70 per cent. were Mahrattas; from 5 to 10 per cent. Brahmins; from 3 to 6 per cent. Rajputs; and from 6 to 8 per cent. Mussulmans. The remaining parts of the D., not included in the above collectorates, are in possession of the rajah of Berar, the nizam, and the rajah of Sattara. The nizam is still nominally one of the most considerable princes of India, but he is merely a protected prince, that is to say, he has surrendered certain rights of sovereignty on condition of receiving protection from the British government, and is bound to defer on particular questions to the advice of a resident at his court. The attention of the Bombay government has for some time been directed to the best means of opening up an easy commercial communication with the D., by which means the cotton, linseed, rice, and grain of this country might find an outlet. The great difficulty which this region presents to the execution of a railway intersecting its length from N to S, is the surmounting the Syhadri range of mountain which stretches between Candeish and Sholapur, and has an average alt. of 2,800 ft. It has been proposed to pass this range at the Malsej Ghat, and to execute a railway running N and S from the Pera river, through Alleh, to Mehuse near Sirur, a distance of about 70 m., with a transverse line from Alleh to Tannah and Bombay, a distance of about 100 m. The trade in cloths, ghee, munjit, baghri, safflower, turmeric, and gums of various kinds, all articles produced in great abundance in the D., but on which the cost of transport presses heavily at present, would also receive great extension from the introduction of a judicious system of trunk-roads and railways, in this district, which has hitherto been comparatively but thinly peopled, and ineffectually cultivated from its possessing no great navigable river, and none of those artificial channels of communication which might have supplied the place of one. The interior provinces of the great table-land, which stretches from the Nerbudda to Cape Comorin, are all of them destitute of that prime necessary of life, salt, which has therefore to be conveyed from the coast; and thousands of Brinjarri bullocks, laden with this article, may constantly be seen traversing the Concan at the rate of 6 or 7 m. a-day, threading the narrow passes of the Ghauts, and on arriving at the summit breaking into separate lines, and taking their way towards every point of the country along the crests of mountains, or beside the beds of rivers, where only Nature's hand has prepared for them a track. A scarcity has never been known to occur on both sides of the peninsula at once: yet at Aurungabad, jowari has sold at 16s. the quarter, while at Punah, only 160 m. distant, it was fetching 64s. and 6d., in consequence of the failure of the crops in the surrounding districts. All that seems to be wanting, to give a great and lasting impulse to trade and commerce in the D., is to enable the different districts reciprocally to exchange their productions. The opening of the great Mirzapore road, as it is usually styled, running between Mirzapore and Jubbulpore, a distance of 200 m., has been of great service, as by means of it the produce of the Nerbudda valley, and of the D., can be transported on wheeled carriages to Mirzapore at any season of the year. This subject is illustrated at considerable length, and with great force and clearness, by a writer in the 35th vol. of the Foreign Quarterly Review.

DECCAN-SHABAZPUR, a deltoid island of Hindostan, in the gulf and prov. of Bengal, at the mouth of the Ganges. It is 30 m. in length, and about 12 m. in breadth; but possesses so little elevation that, during the rainy season, it is almost entirely submerged. It is not inhabited, but it produces rice and salt in great abundance.
DECEIT (CAPE), a bold promontory on the N ings. coast of Chamisso island, 28 m. SSE of Cape Espenberg.

DECEPTION ISLAND, one of the New Shetland group in the S. Pacific, in Slat. 62° 55', W long. 69° 20'. Its diam. is about 8 m. On the SE side is an opening of about 600 ft. wide, which affords an entrance into a circular lake whose diam. is 5 m., around which the land forms a narrow annulus or ring. The depth of this lake in 1829 was 97 fath., with a bottom of cinders; and the surrounding hills of tufa and scoriæ rose to an alt. of 1,800 ft. It is inhabited by penguins, sea-leopards, pintados, and petrels; but exhibits no vegetation except a small kind of lichen.
DECHANI, a small town of Turkey in Europe, 50 m. E of Trikali, on an affluent of lake Carcas.

DECHKIN, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 35 m. N of Orel, district and 10 m. WNW of Mtzensk, on the l. bank of the Oka. Pop. 2,000. It has a port and extensive magazines of salt, and carries on an active trade in corn.

DECHTARZIN, a town of Persia, in Fars, in the district of Komarra, 42 m. W of Shiraz, situated in a fine plain, bounded by the mountains of Pirezen and Aouchanek, and watered by the Zirra, which here forms a lake. Pop. 1,200. The tomb of Mur- 2,025. teza Ali is the only object worthy of note which it contains.
DECHTCHIN, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Volhynia, district and 13 m. SE of Jitomir, on the l. bank of the Gounva.
DECHY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Douai. Pop. 1,279. Beet-root sugar is extensively manufactured here. DED

DECIMOMANNU, a village of the island of Sardinia, in the prov. and 11 m. NW of Cagliari, on the road to Sarrari, near the confluence of the Santatiana and Mannu. Pop. 1,038. A fair is held here once a-year. The environs produce good wine.

DECISION (CAPE), a headland on the SE coast of Russian America, at the S extremity of an island, which lies to the NW of the Prince of Wales archipelago, in N lat. 56° 2'. It was so named on its discovery by Vancouver, who conceived that he had here decided the question with respect to the practicability of a North-West passage.

DECIZE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, arrond. of Nevers. The cant. comprises 16 com., and contained in 1831, 10,714 inhabitants; in 1841, 12,064. The town occupies a picturesque situation, on a high rocky island, formed by the Loire, at the confluence of the Aron, 20 m. SE of Nevers, and at the mouth of the Nivernais canal. Pop. in 1846, 3,849. A suspension bridge connects the island with the main land. The commerce of D., which from the advantages of its situation is extremely active, consists in coal, charcoal, iron, wood, limestone, freestone, gypsum, pottery, cattle, &c. Fairs are held eight times a-year. There are several iron-works, and extensive lime, brick, and tile kilns. The coal-basin of D., which comprises an area of 8,000 hectares, produced in 1839, 295,660 quintals, amounting in value to 280,877 francs, and giving employment to 1,500 persons.
DECKENBACH, a village of the grand-duchy of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the prov. of Upper Hesse, bail. and 3 m. WSW of Homberg. Pop. 290, chiefly weavers.
DECKENFROND, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of Schwarzwald, bail. and 7 m. SE of Calw. Pop. 900.
DECKERTOWN, a village of Wantage township, Sussex co., in the state of New Jersey, U.S., 87 m. N of Trenton, consisting in 1840 of about 20 dwell-

DECKINGEN, or DEGGINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Danube, bail. and near Geislingen, on the Fils. Pop. in 1840, 1,681 (Cath.). Great numbers of the inhabitants of this locality annually emigrate to other districts as masons and plasterers, returning in winter to the employments of basket and spindle making.

DECKNALL, or Dakshinalaga, a fortified town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, capital of a tributary zemindary, in the district and 40 m. N of Cuttack. The zemindary, comprising an area 112 m. from E to W, and about 87 m. in breadth, produces rice, sugar, cotton, iron, timber, dammer, wax, and honey. In 1814 its annual tribute amounted to 4,780 rupees.
DECUMEN, a parish of Somerset, 5 m. E of Dunster, on the coast, S of Watchet. Area 3,250 acres. Pop. 2,660.
DEDA, a town of Hungary, in the comitat of Bihar, 30 m. NE of Grosswardein, near the N bank of the Berettyo, and on the confines of Transylvania.
DEDDINGTON, a parish of Oxfordshire, 16 m. NW of Oxford, and 69 m. NW of London, near the Oxford canal, bounded on the E by the Cherwell, and on the N by the Snore. Area 3,990 acres. Pop.
DEDELER, a town of Caramania, in the sanj. and 35 m. N of Konieh, on the road from that town to Angora.
DEDELERI, a town of Albania, in the sanj. and 70 m. SE of Monastir, and 10 m. NE of Cojani, near the l. bank of the Inji-Cara-su.
DEDHAM, a parish of Essex, 33 m. NW of Manningtree, near the London and Norwich railway, and on the S bank of the Stour, which is here crossed by a bridge. Area 2,660 acres. Pop. 1,787.

DEDHAM, a township of Hancock co., in the state of Maine, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 455.—Also a township, capital of Norfolk co., in the state of Massachusetts, 13 m. SSW of Boston, watered by Charles and Neponset rivers, and one of their branches, and intersected by the Boston and Providence railroad, a branch of which runs to the village which is situated on Charles river. Pop. of township, 3,920. It had in 1840, 12 stores, 3 woollen factories, 2 cotton factories with 4,200 spindles, 2 tanneries, 4 grist and 4 saw mills, a paper factory, and 2 printingoffices.
DEDIOUKTIMA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Perm, in the environs of which are extensive saline springs.

DEDROO-OYA, or DEDURU, a river of Ceylon, which takes its rise to the W of Nalande, at the base of the Tumpani mountains; is joined by the Kospotté, Mongra-oyas, and some other small streams from the S; runs WNW; and after a total course of about 60 m. falls into the gulf of Manaar, 2 m. N of Chilaw. Its tidal flow and ebb are remarkably irregular.
DEDWORTH, a hamlet in the p. of New Windsor, Berkshire, 2 m. SW of Windsor, S of the Thames. Pop. 101.

DEE, a river which rises on the S frontier of Merionethshire, at the foot of the Berwyn mountains, and flows NE 4 m. into the lake of Bala or Llyn- Tegid, which is generally considered as the source of the river. The waters of this mountain-lake are discharged at its NE corner by a stream of considerable size and depth, which in its winding course NE to Corwen is soon increased by other mountain-streams, as the Treweryn or Glettwr and the Alwen. The river so far flows in an open valley, and its course is not rapid till it reaches Corwen, where it begins to descend from the mountain table-land of N. Wales. In 10 m. from Corwen to Trevor it falls about 300 ft.; and its course in this part is consequently rapid. Below Trevor it enters the plain, crosses Denbighshire from W to E, through the vale of Llangollen, and runs with numerous windings upwards of 30 m. to the tideway of Chester, receiving in Denbighshire the Rhaiadyr, the Alwen, the Ceiriog, and the Alyn. It then enters Cheshire near Aldford, and takes a N course. At Chester the D. is about 100 yds. wide, and flows in a straight artificial channel or tidal canal, capable of admitting ships of 600 tons, along the marshes for about 8 m. to where it enters its proper estuary. Here it enlarges into a frith 3 m. across, which at high water forms a noble arm of the sea; but at ebb-tide is covered with sand and ooze, through which the river runs in a narrow and insignificant low-water channel. It enters the Irish sea 15 m. below this, between the island of Great Helbre on the N, and the Point of Ayre on the S, where the estuary is about 6 m. wide. In its natural state the D. is wholly unnavigable; but by means of a weir at Chester, the water is dammed up so as to allow small boats to ascend 2 or 3 m. above Chester. The whole course of this river from Bala-pool to the beginning of the estuary may be about 60 m. The D. is subject to frequent and sudden floods; and, from its rapid and unequal fall throughout the greater part of its course, it is for the most part unfit for navigation till it reaches Chester, and serves chiefly for the discharge of the draining waters of the country, and for working mills. The entrance to the D. commences at Chester bar or flats, 27 m. below Chester, and 12 m. W of the Black-rock lighthouse or entrance to the Mersey. The S channel across the bar is 1 m. wide, and has 12 ft. at low water of spring tides. The N entrance commences at Helbre gap.

DEE, a large river in Aberdeenshire, which takes its rise in Braemar, in the bosom of the Cairngorm group, chiefly in two head-streams. The northern of these rises in a clear spring, at an alt. of 4,060 ft., between the ridge of Braeriach on the W, and Loch- Aven on the E, and, running a rapid course nearly due S through Glen-Garrochory for 2 m., is there joined by another small stream called the Guisachan, flowing from the W, when it assumes the name of the Dee. Flowing on in the same direction through a deep glen for 4 m. further, it receives the Geauly or Giouly, its S head - branch, at Dubrach. The Geauly has its source at the base of Cairneilar, whence also issues the Tarf, a branch of the Tilt, which flows S to the Tay, and the Feshie, which running N, falls into the Spey. After the junction of the two waters, the D. becomes a considerable stream, and bends its course, through a rocky channel, ENE, shortly after which it forms a cascade, or a series of small falls, known as the linn of Dee, where it flows through a deep chasm in mica slate rocks. About 11 m. below the linn, some signs of cultivation begin to appear on the banks of the D.; but it soon after enters Mar-forest, through which it flows to Castleton of Braemar, a distance of 11 m., receiving in its course the Lui and the Quoich, from the N; and the Inverey and the Clunie from the S. From Castleton, the D. pursues its course through the Mar and Invercauld forests to the bridge of Ballater, about 11 m. above which it is joined by a large affluent, the water of Gairden, from the N, and by the Muick from the S. Passing Pannanich and Dee-castle, the D. flows E through a gradually widening though still narrow valley, receiving numerous small tributaries on both banks, and forcing its way through an alluvium composed of rolled masses of coarse and fine granular, grey and red granite, gneiss, porphyry, primitive greenstone, and hornblende. About 13 m. below Kincardine O'Neil, where Potarch bridge crosses the D., there is a magnificent vein of red felspar porphyry, traversing gneiss, and varying in breadth from 6 to 20 ft. Below Potarch, the D. enters Kincardineshire, through which it flows eastward for about 12 m., receiving in this part of its course the Aven or Feugh. From the point where it touches on the SW corner of Drumoak p. in Aberdeenshire, till its confluence with the sea at Aberdeen, it forms the dividing line betwixt Aberdeenshire and Kincardineshire. Its banks, throughout this distance, are rather tame and unpicturesque, - the hills lumpish and heath-covered, and presenting few cliffs,—and the haughs narrow, except for the last 5 or 6 m. of its course. Its total length of course is about 88 m.; and its tributaries drain nearly 1,000 sq. m. of country. Its fall from the linn of Dee to the sea, a distance of 72 m., is 1,190 ft. Its mouth forms the entrance to the harbour of Aberdeen, which is an artificial basin between New Aberdeen and the suburb of Footdee on the N, and the river D. on the S. This river abounds with salmon; the most valuable fishings in Scotlandthe Tay and the Spey excepted—are on it. The D. has much decreased in size within the last 40 years, owing, doubtless in great measure, to the improvement and drainage of the country.

DEE, a river in Kirkcudbrightshire, traversing the whole length of the stewartry, and dividing it into two nearly equal parts. Its sources are about a dozen rills, some pursuing an independent course, and some passing through Long Loch or Loch Dee, and all arising in the broad mountain-range which separates Kirkcudbrightshire from Carrick. The highest, and strictly the parent-stream, rises about a mile from the boundary, and before receiving the MOHUMMED KAHN, NEAR A. Pallerton & Co Landon & Edinburgh DEEG. Da. Ser TAND - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -94 10 surplus waters of Loch Dee, flows circuitously about 6 m. It then flows 17 m. NE, receiving numerous rills from the uplands in its course. Falling into Loch Ken, it usurps the titles and the tributes of the larger and beautiful river by which that lake is formed; and thence over a distance of 9 or 10 m. describes the arc of a circle, bending round from SE to SW; the latter direction it maintains over 13 m., till it falls into the Solway frith, forming an estuary at first 3 of a m., and afterwards 12 m., of average breadth. Its entire course is about 46 m., and it is navigable 7 m. from its mouth.

DEE, a river of the cos. of Meath and Louth. It rises between Moynalty and Nobber, and has an E course of about 21 m., through the Meath baronies of Morgallion and Slane, and along the Louth barony of Ardee, to a common embouchure with the Glyde or Lagan, on the S side of Dundalk bay, 41 m. W by N of Dunavy-point. It is, almost throughout, a dull, tame stream, flowing between flat banks.
DEE, a river of Van Diemen's Land, which issues from Echo lake, on the W confines of the district of Clyde, runs SSE, and unites with the Derwent, on the district of New Norfolk, 10 m. E of Hamilton.
DEEBAR. See PORT HACKING.

DEEG, a town and fortress of Hindostan, in the Rajput states, 12 m. NNW of Bhurtpur, 50 m. NW of Agra. Extensive embankments defend it from the torrents which descend from the hills. It was formerly a place of considerable importance, and contains the ruins of several handsome edifices. It sustained a siege by Nudkiff Khan in 1776, and again in 1805 by Lord Lake.

DEEGOA, a town of Central Africa, 20 m. S by W of Alla in Bornu, and governed by a sultan, subject to the sheikh of Bornu. Denham estimated its pop. at about 30,000. With the exception of the immediate neighbourhood of the town, the country is here thickly wooded.

DEEL, or ASKEATON, a river of co. Limerick, rising in the mountains of Orrery, in Cork, and running N to the Shannon, into which it flows, after a course of about 26 m., at a point 7 m. below the influx of the Maig. It is a tidal stream, and navigable to Askeaton.—Also a rivulet in co. Mayo, flowing into Lough Conn.
DEELA, a village of Afghanistan, on the Ghuzni, a little above its entrance into Lake Abistadeh, in about N lat. 32° 43'.
DEEOLEA, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Ajmere, between Missirabad and Dabla. It has a mud rampart and a ruinous fort.
DEEP, a small port of Prussia, in Pomerania, at the embouchure of the Rega. Vessels which have not water to ascend to Treptow discharge their cargoes here.
DEEP CREEK, a village in Norfolk co., in the state of Virginia, U. S., 114 m. SE of Richmond, at the termination of Dismal Swamp canal.

DEEP RIVER, a section of the main channel of the Ottawa river, in Upper Canada, about 25 m. in length, the head of which is about 150 m. from Bytown.—Also a river of N. Carolina, U. S., rising in the co. of Guilford, and flowing through Randolph, Moore, and Chatham cos., to Cape Fear, which it joins on the r. bank, after a sinuous course of 120 m. -Also a small tributary of Connecticut river, in Middlesex co., in the state of Connecticut.—Also a village in Lake co., in Iowa, 148 m. NNE of Indianapolis.
DEEP SPRING, a village in Hawkins co., in the state of Tennessee, U. S., 256 m. E by N of Nashville.
DEEP WATER, a village in Van Buren co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. 261. DEE
DEEP WELL, a village in Iredell co., in N. Carolina, U. S., 137 m. W of Raleigh.
DEEPING-FEN, an extra-parochial district in Lincolnshire, 6 m. SW of Spalding. Area 16,290 acres. Pop. 973. The fen comprises upwards of 30,000 acres. See LINCOLNSHIRE.
DEEPING-ST .- JAMES, or DEEPING-EAST, a parish of Lincolnshire, 1 m. E by N of Deeping-Market. Area 6,470 acres. Pop. 1,733.
DEEPING-GATE, a hamlet in the p. of Maxey, Northamptonshire, 1 m. SE of Deeping - Market. Pop. 201.
DEEPING-MARKET, a parish and town in Lincolnshire, 93 m. NNW of London, on the N bank of the Welland. Area of p. 1,290 acres. Pop. 1,219.
DEEPING-WEST, a parish of Lincolnshire, 25 m. WSW of Market-Deeping. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 306.

DEER (OLD), a parish partly in Aberdeenshire, partly in Banff. Pop. in 1801, 3,552; in 1841, 4,453. It is intersected by the Deer and the Strichen, two rivulets which unite to form the Ugie; and contains the villages of Old D., Stewartfield, and Fetterangus. -Old D. village is situated on the N bank of the D., 101 m. W of Peterhead. In its vicinity are the remains of a once extensive abbey.

DEER (NEW), an extensive parish in the NE of Aberdeenshire. Pop. in 1801, 2,984; in 1841, 3,756. The D. river takes its rise in this p., and after a course of about 16 m. unites with the Strichen as noticed in the preceding article.
DEER (GREAT and LITTLE), two islets off the SW coast of Celebes; the former 16 m. W by S of Macassar town; the latter 8 m. W by N of Macassar.

DEER-CREEK, a village in Mercer co., in Pennsylvania, U. S., 236 m. WNW of Harrisburg.—Also a v. in Madison co., in Ohio. Pop. 545.—Also a v. in Livingston co., in Michigan, 61 m. W of Detroit. -Also a town in Pickaway co., in Ohio, on a creek of the same name. Pop. 1,374.—Also a small stream in Harford co., in Maryland, an affluent of the Susquehanna.
DEEREN, a rivulet of cos. Wicklow and Carlow, rising on the S side of Lugnaquilla, and flowing 14 m. SW to the Slaney.

DEERFIELD, a township in Rockingham co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., 19 m. SE of Concord. Pop. 1,950. In the N part is Pleasant pond, a large body of water having its outlet into Suncook river.—Also a township in Franklin co., in Massachusetts, 95 m. W by N of Boston, intersected by a river of the same name, an affluent of the Connecticut. Pop. 1,912.—Also a township in Oneida co., in the state of New York, 4 m. N of Utica. Pop. 3,020.—Also a township in Cumberland co., New Jersey, watered by Cohansey creek and Muddy river. Pop. 2,621.—Also a township in Tioga co., in Pennsylvania, 13 m. NW of Wellsborough. Pop. 615 .- Also a v. in Warren co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 450. -Also a township in Portage co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,184.—Also a township in Warren co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,871.—Also a township in Morgan co., in Ohio. Pop. 1,235.—Also a township in Augusta co., in Virginia.—Also a township in Ross co., in Ohio, 44 m. S of Columbus.—Also a township in Van Buren co., in Missouri. Pop. 301.—Also a township in Livingston co., in Michigan. Pop. 440. - Also a river in Massachusetts, rising in Vermont, and after a course of 50 m. flowing into the Connecticut.
DEERFIELDSTREET, a village in Deerfield township, in New Jersey, U. S., 62 m. SSW of Trenton.
DEERFIELDVILLE, a village in Warren co., in Ohio, 89 m. SE of Columbus, on the N bank of Little Miami.
DEERHURST, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. | SW of Tewkesbury, on the E bank of the Severn. Area 2,390 acres. Pop. 937. It gives the title of Viscount to the Earl of Coventry.
DEERING, a township in Hillsboro' co., in New Hampshire, U. S., 25 m. SW of Concord, intersected by Contoocrook river. Pop. 1,124.

DEER ISLAND, an island in co. Clare, near the W shore of the estuary of the Fergus, 3 m. NNE of Kildysart. It is about 1 m. in length.—Also an island of the state of Maine, U.S., on the E side of Penobscot bay, which, with 2 or 3 adjacent small islands, had a pop. of 2,841 in 1840. On this island occurs an enormous mass of serpentine rock, said to be identical with the verd-antique marble. It is of a deep olive-green colour, with lines of asbestos, and spots of yellow diallage, and has been projected through the granite.-Also an island of New Brunswick, in Passamaquoddy bay. It is 12 m. long, and 3 m. broad. On its S side are several harbours for small vessels and boats, sheltered by a number of smaller islands. The pop., amounting to about 2,500, are chiefly employed in fishing, DEERNESS AND ST. ANDREW'S, a parish in the island of Pomona, in Orkney. The district of Deerness forms a peninsula, which, from the Mullhead to the isthmus that divides it from the district of St. Andrews, is in length upwards of 4 m., and varies in breadth from 1 to 3 m, as the large and beautifully winding harbour of Deer-sound enlarges or contracts. This harbour runs nearly in the direction of NE and SW; it is 4 m. long, and from 1 to 23 m. broad. Its entrance is from the N; and as it is surrounded with land on every side, and has a bottom of clay mixed with sand and a sufficient depth of water, it constitutes an excellent harbour. The pop. of the p. in 1801 was 660; in 1831, 661; in 1841, 777, mostly fishermen.

DEERPARK, a township in Orange co., in the state of New York, U. S., 38 m. W of Newburgh. It is intersected by the Neversink and Bush's-kill, tributaries of the Delaware; and by the Delaware and Hudson canal, and New York and Erie railroad. Pop. 1,607.
DEER-RIVER-FALLS, a village in Franklin co., in the state of New York, U.S., 197 m. N by W of Albany.
DEERSVILLE, a village in Harrison co., in Ohio, 109 m. E by N of Columbus.

DEE'S, or Des, a town of Transylvania, in the district of Bselso-Szolnok, 27 m. NNE of Klausenburg, at the confluence of the Kis-Szamos and Nagy- Szamos. Pop. 5,530. In the environs are extensive salt-mines, producing annually 50,000 quintals of salt. The mineral lies in waving horizontal beds, above beds of rolled flint.
DEESA, or DISA, a town of Hindostan, in Guzerat, in the district of Patanoad, in N lat. 24° 9′, 88 m. NNW of Ahmedabad.

DEFAFAUNGH (JEBEL), a rocky isolated hill, rising on the r. bank of the White Nile, under the parallel of 11° 5', in the Dinka territory. Werne supposes it to be a burnt-out volcano. Its summit forms an oval terrace, stretching NW and SE. The prospect from the top presents some Dinka villages towards the S, a distant chain of mountains on the SE, and another towards the E. All these objects seem to be isolated in the immeasurable plain. The Arabs and Turks, mistaking its scattered blocks of lava for ruins, call it BERBA or BIRBE.
DEFFORD, a chapelry in Worcestershire, 3 m. SW of Pershore. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 422. The Birmingham and Gloucester railway has a station here, 33 m. from Birmingham, and 57½ m. from Bristol. DEH

DEFYNOCK, or DEVYNOCK, a parish in Breconshire, 8 m. W by S of Brecon, intersected by an affluent of the Usk, and containing the hamlets of Cray, Glyntawe, Glynn, Maescar, and Senny. Pop. in 1801, 1,694; in 1841, 1,927.
DEGAGNAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lot, cant. of Salviac, 5 m. SSW of Gourdon. Pop. 1,994.
DEGENFELD, a village of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, bailiwick of Gmünd. Pop. 342.
DEGERLOCH, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, 4 m. S of Stuttgard. Pop. 1,256.
DEGET, an island of Denmark, in the Cattegat, near the NE coast of Jutland, 1 m. E of Frederickshavn. There is a redoubt on this island for the protection of the mouth of the Rhede.

DEGGENDORF, or Deckendorf, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Under-Bavaria, on the l. bank of the Rhine, 42 m. NW of Passau. Pop. 2,550. It has foundries, distilleries, and corn, paper, and oilmills; and carries on a considerable trade in cattle. It was greatly ravaged by fire in 1822.
DEGGINGEN, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, SE of Nördlingen. Pop. 644.

DEGHIMIR, a village of Nubia, on the E bank of the Nile, 21 m. above Philæ, and 3 m. above Siala, on the r. bank of the river, which here has a direction a point to the E of N. The mountains here retire from the sides of the river, and the banks where any soil occurs are cultivated.
DEGHITCHE, or DEGITCHE, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Vilna, district and 50 m. W by S of Rossiena.
DEGLIA, a village and district of Sicily, 9 m. SW of Caltanisetta. Pop. 2,260.
DEGNIZLU. See DENIZLU.

DEGO, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 18 m. S of Acqui, on the l. bank of the Bormida. In the campaign of 1796, Buonaparte defeated the Austro-Sardinians under Beaulieu in a series of splendid manœuvres near this place, which is important as guarding the route by Acqui into Lombardy.

DEGOMBA, or DAGUMBA, a district of SW Africa, lying to the NE of Ashantee. Its cap. is Yandy, on the N confines of the great forest of Tonuma, 21 days NNE of Cumassie, the cap. of Ashantee, and 13 days NW of Abomey, the cap. of Dahomey.

DEGUE, or DIGUE (LA), one of the Seychelles or Mahé islands, 21 m. SSE of Praslin, in S lat. 4° 21', W long. 55° 49'. Its area is estimated at 2,000 acres. Turtles are abundant on this island, and form the chief food of the inhabitants.

DEH, or DIH, a Persian word signifying 'village;' and a frequent affix in oriental topography, as in the following instances. It is not unfrequently placed last in the compound term: as in Agdeh, Shuturdeh, Abideh.
DEHAK, or Dohak, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, 50 m. NW of Ispahan, on the road to Hamadan.—Also a v. in the prov. of Laristan, 15 m. NW of Lar.
DEH-BEID, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, 30 m. NNW of Moorgaub.
DEH-DAUNA, a village of Persia, in Khorasan, 22 m. N of Turshiz.
DEH-DOMBA, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, 20 m. SSE of Jarom, on the route to Lar.

DEHESA, or Dahesa, a village of Chili, in the dep. and 15 m. E of Santiago, on a stream of the same name, one of the head-streams of the Maypu, and on the route leading from the valley of Tupungato, around the N side of the peak of Tupungato, into Chili by the D. pass.
DEH-FATEH-KHAN, a village of Afghanistan, 14 m. S by W of Candahar.
DEH-GIRDU, a village of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, on the elevated plain of Oojan, 30 m. W of Abadeh, at the head of a gloomy valley surrounded by dreary hills.
DEH-HAJI, a walled village of Afghanistan, on the route from the Kojuk pass to Candahar. Pop. about 2,000.
DEHI-AKUN, a village of Afghanistan, 50 m. WSW of Candahar, on the r. bank of the Urghundab.
DEHI-DERVESHAN, a town of Afghanistan, in Gurmsir, on the l. bank of the Helmund, in N lat. 31° 40′. .
DEHINDA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Berar, 33 m. SW of Ellichpur.
DEHINGH. See DIHONG.
DEHI-NU, a village of Afghanistan, 50 m. NW of Herat.—Also a v. of Bokhara, 110 m. NNE of Balkh.
DEHI-UR, a village of Persia, in Luristan, 16 farsangs W of Behbehan, on the road to Shuster.
DEH-KEIR, a village of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, 20 m. S by W of Darabgerd.

DEHKERGAN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Azerdbijan, 28 m. SSW of Tabriz, in the rich and fertile district of Maragha. It is a place of considerable antiquity. Extensive gardens surround each house; and a large quantity of fruit is sent from this place and the vicinity to the Tabriz market.
DEH-KEPUK, a village of Afghanistan, 5 m. NW of Cabul, at the W extremity of the lake of Cabul. Pop. 500.
DEH-KUNDI, a village of Afghanistan, on the r. bank of the Helmund, 98 m. N by E of Candahar.
DEHLI. See DELHI.
DEH-MULLAH, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Khorasan, 15 m. SW of Shahrud.

DEHMYT (WADI), a cluster or a series rather of scattered hamlets in Nubia, extending about 4 m. along the E bank of the Nile, 24 hours S of Debod. To the S of D., the mountain-ridge which borders the river as far as Wadi-Halfa is composed of sandstone. From Assuan to D. it is granite.
DEH-NUSSIR, a village of Afghanistan, 12 m. N of Khash.

DEHR, or DYUR, a village of the Punjab, on the r. bank of the Sutledge, in N lat. 30° 42'. It has a castle on an eminence overhanging the river, which Vigne found here, in the month of March, to have a smooth but quick stream, about 200 yards in breadth. DEHR, DERR, or DERRI, the capital of Nubia, and usual residence of its kashif or governor, 15 m. NE of Ibrim, in N lat. 22° 44'. Burckhardt, who visited it in 1813, describes it as a v. of about 200 houses overshadowed by a grove of palm-trees. Mrs. Romer, who saw it in Dec. 1845, characterizes it as "a collection of mud hovels." Mr. St. John in 1832 describes it as by far the most agreeable place he had seen in the valley of the Nile. The houses, he says, are well-built with clay or sun-dried bricks, and generally constructed in the form of square towers, with a large court in front, surrounded by high walls. The streets, he says, are wide and scrupulously clean, and the environs present neat walled gardens filled with orange, date, and acacia trees. D. has therefore, it would appear, vastly declined under the government of the pasha of Egypt's lieutenants. -The inhabitants of D. are supposed to be the descendants of a number of Bosnian soldiers who were established in Nubia by Sultan Selim I. in 1517; and they still preserve in a great measure their European features, and comparatively fair complexion. - On the declivity of a rocky hill just behind the town are the remains of a temple of remote antiquity, which has been excavated in the face of the sandstone cliff. -The valley immediately S of D. is fertile and ^ DEH beautiful. Here palm-trees, planted in straight lines, form stately avenues extending from the river to the mountains; and fields of wheat, tobacco, and cotton, alternate with each other, to the extremity of the wadi or the frontiers of Wadi Ibrim.

DEHRA, a town of Hindostan, the cap. of the territory known as the Dehra Dun, in N lat. 30° 18' 51", E long. 78° 2', at an alt. of 2,369 ft. above sealevel. In 1827 it contained 518 houses, and 2,126 inhabitants, exclusive of the local corps permanently cantoned here, which amounts to about 900 men bearing arms, besides their families and servants.

DEHRA-DUN, a valley of India, bounded on the NE by Gurwhal; on the SE by the Ganges; on the NW by the Jumna. Measured from Hurdwar to the Jumna, opposite Kalsi, the valley is 57 m. in length, and about 15 m. wide at its broadest part near the centre. But measuring to the tops of the bounding ranges, and embracing some small and recently annexed territories, the whole collectorship has an area of 740 sq. m., of which only 29 sq. m. are under cultivation. The Ganges enters the district at Tapubun, and leaves the valley at Hurdwar, after flowing about 17 m. through the Dun or 'valley.' It is here a rapid stream, full of islands, and with difficulty fordable. The Jumna's course bounds the D. for about 18 m. There are many stream-courses which are dry after rain; but the Sohun is the largest permanent stream wholly within the D. The only place approaching to anything like a town within the D. is Dehra; but there are about 15 villages, each containing 50 houses and upwards. An attempt at a census made in 1823 returned 4,962 houses, and a pop. of 20,179. Elephants, tigers, leopards, cheetas, black bears, and striped hyenas, roam in the forests. The tigers and leopards are very numerous. There are also caracalls, lynxes, foxes, jackalls, wild dogs, wild cats, civet cats, weasels, bandicoots, monkeys, rats, mice, and deer. Eagles, vultures, kites, hawks, crows, and ravens, are among the birds of prey. The peacock, pheasant, black and grey partridge, jungle fowl, quail, curlew, snipe, and woodcock, are among the game birds. Parroquets, pigeons, and doves, abound. The boa exists in the woods, and snakes, scorpions, guanas, and chameleons, are numerous.—The average temp. of the year 1827-8 was 70°8; lowest 35°; highest 102°.—Rice, wheat, the sugar-cane, Indian corn, and cotton, are cultivated. The pomegranate, lemon, guava, and plantain, thrive admirably. There are few manufactures: blankets, cotton cloth, mats, baskets, and earthen pots, comprise the whole. A few boats are built on the banks of the Ganges and the Jumna.—The produce of the D. carried to the plains consists of timber, bamboos, khut or terra Japonica, rice, and grain. From the hills are brought coarse blankets, rice, ginger, turmeric, red pepper, hooka pipes, booj or birch bark, honey, wax, lac, gums, and dyeing and medicinal herbs, roots, and mosses. The merchants of Dehra deal chiefly with those of Saharunpur.—The total collection of the D. for 1827-8 was about 26,164 rupees, exclusive of the abkari, which might yield 1,802 r.

DEHRA-JAM-KA, a decayed town and deserted English factory in Sinde, founded in the early part of the 18th cent. on the Mugrah branch of the Indus, in N lat. 24° 10'. The factory was afterwards removed to Shahbunda.
DEHRENTHAL, a village of Brunswick, in the circle and 9 m. S of Holzminden. Pop. 517.

DEHRI-KOTI, or DERA-GHAIBI, a town of Cutch- Gundava, at the base of the Hala range, in about N lat. 27° 38', near a branch or canal from the Indus to the Nari. It comprises a few mud houses, chiefly the dwellings of Hindus, and a large number of huts. Beluchis.

DEHRUNG, a principality of Assam, divided from N Camrup by the Burronuddi, and having the Brahmaputra on the S. It forms a square territory measuring about 30 m. each way. It is fertile and well-cultivated. Its principal towns are Monguldi, Simuagown, Tangazugonigown, Paoniagown, Arringgown, Dunigown, Batacusihath, and Ataringhiagown.
DEH-ZANGI, a town of Afghanistan, in the district of Ghoraut, 140 m. NE of Ghuzni.
DEH-ZIRGARAN, a village of Afghanistan, 20 m. NW of Cabul, near the Furza, an affluent of the Punchshir.
DEIDESHEIM, a village of Bavaria, in the Palatinat circle, 12 m. WNW of Spires. Pop. 2,227.
DEIFAN, a village of Arabia, in the prov. of Yemen, 50 m. N of Sanaa.

DEIGHTON, a chapelry in North Allerton p., in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. N by E of N Allerton. Area 2,210 acres. Pop. 132.—Also a township in the p. of Escrick, E. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. S by E of York. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 185.
DEIGHTON-KIRK, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. N of Wetherby. Area 2,470 acres. Pop. in 1841, 539, of whom 371 were in the township of Kirk D., and 168 in that of North D.
DEILINGEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the Black forest circle, on the Heuberge. Pop. 772.
DEIMOLI, a lofty conical mountain in the Somali territory of NE Africa. It is apparently of limestone, thickly wooded, and a great resort for beasts of prey.

DEINACH, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black forest, 9 m. NW of Herrenberg. Pop. 400. There are acidulous mineral springs here, and baths. Their prevailing ingredients are carbonic acid, with double its quantity of carbonate of soda and carbonate of lime. Temp. about 45°.
DEINE, a river of Prussia, in the circle of Konigsberg, which branches off from the Pregel near Tapian, and flows into the Curische-haff to the N of Labiau.

DEIR, a town and district of Eastern Syria, on the r. bank of the Euphrates. It is 6 or 7 hours below El Braylia, by the river, and 8 days from Aleppo, by caravan. It contains about 1,500 houses, and is inhabited by Bohabur Arabs. Opposite to the town is an island of the same name. The Anizeh and Zoba Arabs intervene between D. and Aleppo, and the pashas of Aleppo and of Bagdad have long been unable to enforce obedience from them; while Ibrahim Pasha occupied D., however, these tribes were kept completely overawed.

DEIR-ANTONIOS, a monastery in the Eastern desert of Upper Egypt, 17 m. from the Red sea, and 76 m. from Benisuef. It is enclosed within a wall from 30 to 40 ft. high. The only entrance is by a trap-door; an insulated tower, defended by a drawbridge, is the keep of the fortification. The interior resembles the confusion of an Arab v. The number of monks was stated to Mr. Wilkinson, in 1823, to be 63. This place is interesting as having been the place of abode and of sepulture of the founder of Monachism. About 14 m. to the SE is another convent called Deir-Bolos; and about 10 m. NE by N is Deïr-Bekheit. An interesting account of these different monasteries is given by Mr. Wilkinson in the 2d vol. of the Geog. Journal .- Deir, or Dayr, signifies 'a convent.'

DEIRA, a village and stream of Persia, in the district of Kirmanshah. The v. is 10 m. S of Sar- Puli-Zohab, on the r. bank of the stream, in a plain 4 m. in length, and 2 m. in breadth, celebrated for its excellent herbage. It is inhabited by about 150 resident Kalhur families, and affords kishlak or win- DEL It is the residence of the chief of the Chandi tribe of I their habits. - The stream, flowing NW, joins the Holwan at Mulla-Yakub.
DEIRAH, or SIYAF, a fort in Cutch-Gundava, in a fertile valley of about 6 m. in extent, in the Bugti hills, 64 m. E of Phulaji.
DEIR-EL-KAMAR. See AKHMAR.
DEIR-IBRAHIM. See DERUG.
DEIR-SAFERAN, a town of Turkey in Asia, 9 m. NW of Mardin, in the pash. of Diarbekir. It is the residence of the Jacobite patriarch.
DEIRUT, a town of Lower Egypt, 15 m. S of Rosetta, in the prov. of Fouah, on the l. bank of the W branch of the Nile.
DEISENDORF, a village in the archd. of Austria, in the circle and 12 m. W of Salzburg, on an affluent of the Salza.
DEISSEL, a village of Hessen-Cassel, in the bail. of Trendelburg, on the l. bank of the Dimel, 1 m. N of Hof-Geismar. Pop. 900.
DEIZISAU, a village of Wurtemberg, in the bail. and 8 m. SE of Eslingen, on the l. bank of the Neckar. Pop. 827.

DE KALB, a central county of the state of Georgia, U. S., comprising an area consisting of elevated table-land of 360 sq. m., watered by the Chattahoochee and Œmulgee rivers, and intersected by the Georgia railroad. Pop. in 1830, 10,042; in 1840, 10,467. Its capital is Decatur.—Also a county of the state of Alabama, containing a superficies, generally hilly, of 1,500 sq. m., bounded on the NW by the Tennessee, and intersected by Will's creek, a branch of Coosa river, which runs through a fine level tract. Pop. in 1840, 5,929. Its capital is Lebanon.—Also a central county of the state of Tennessee, possessing an area of 275 m., bordered on the N and W by the Cumberland mountains. The S consists of level wastes. The soil, although scanty, is in other parts tolerably fertile in tobacco and grain. Pop. 5,868. Its capital is Smithville.—Also a county of the state of Indiana, containing a superficies of 365 sq. m., watered by St. Joseph's branch of Maumee river and several creeks. Pop. 1,968. Its capital is Auburn. -Also a township of St. Lawrence co., in the state of New York, 195 m. NNW of Albany. It is generally hilly, and is watered by Oswegatchie river and its tributaries. It contains marble and iron-ore. Pop. 1,531. The village is on the E side of the Oswegatchie river, and contained in 1840, 150 inhabitants.—Also a village of Kemper co., in the state of Mississippi, 106 m. ENE of Jackson, and 2 m. S of Sacarnochee creek.-Also a village of Lewis co., in the state of Virginia, 313 m. WNW of Richmond, on the N bank of Little Kanawha river.
DEKKAN. See DECCAN.
DEKKEH. See DAKKEH.
DEKNAL, a town of Hindostan, in the district and 40 m. N of Cuttack.

DELAGOA, or Dalagoa, a territory on the E coast of Africa, situated around a bay of the same name, between the parallels of 25° and 27°, but of which the interior boundaries and extent are altogether uncertain. It is under the power of different chiefs; and no doubt is distinguished into various districts, but our knowledge of it is in great part still limited to the coasts. The country around the bay appears to be thus divided. To the S lies the Maputa or oil country; to the SW, Temby; to the NW, Mattoll; and to the N, Mabota. To the S of Maputa, the country appears to be in the hands of a tribe of warlike Caffres called Zulus. In the bay lie two or three islands, and sand-banks. The largest island, called Inyack (g), is intersected by the parallel of 26°, which also intersects the S part of the estuary of English river. Off the mouth of King George ter-quarters to about 400 more who are nomadic in | river lies a long narrow island called Shefeen island (h), off which shoals extend in various directions. The SE side of the bay is formed by a long narrow peninsular projection terminating in Cape Colatto (i), between which and the S point of Inyack island is a channel of somewhat less than 1 m. in breadth. Three rivers empty themselves into the bay. The Manice, or King George river (a), which is the most northern, has been explored for 50 m. The Machavanna, or Maputa (b), which is the most southern, is navigable 30 leagues from its mouth by boats which draw 6 ft. of water. The Delagoa, or English river (c), which is the central river, is navigable for nearly 200 m. by large boats, and for more than 40 m. by vessels which draw 12 ft. of water. It has a bar with about 15 ft. on it at low water; about 2 m. up the river, vessels lie in sufficient depth, and safe from every wind. This river is, in reality, the estuary of three rivers, called the Temby (d), the Dundas (e), and the Mattoll (f), all of which have their sources at about a day's journey from their common estuary, and run into English river at little more than 5 m. above the fort. Their shores are generally bordered by an extensive muddy flat, gradually rising towards higher land. D. bay is a commanding expanse of water, with several woody islands. It extends about 60 m. from N to S, and 21 m. from E to W. Its navigation for vessels of burden is intricate, but this difficulty surmounted, it has natural advantages of which few places can boast; and is much frequented by whalers, particularly Americans, who come here for supplies. The entrance to English river is not without something of a picturesque character, in the appearance of the v. and fort, and the sand-hills covered with calabash trees. Had the slave-trade never existed here, and the means of the Portuguese been diverted to other pursuits, this part of Caffraria would probably have become their most valuable possession, from the richness of the country in the back-ground, the quantity of ivory, gums, and other valuable commodities which the natives bring in from the interior, and the number of cetaceous animals with which the bay abounds. The number of whales found in the bay at peculiar seasons, as well as throughout the Mozambique channel, is incredible. They are smaller than those met with in the Greenland seas, but were they only to yield from 10 to 15 tons each, their plentifulness would compensate for diminution of size. II. -The soil is fertile, and productive of rice and maize, which are sown in December or January. The dry season lasts from April to October. Various species of fruit, and sugar-canes, are plentiful. The forests are inhabited by the panther, rhinoceros, buffalo, zebra, antelope, wild hog, hare, and rabbit; tame cattle are abundant; and wild geese, ducks, quails, partridges, Guinea-hens, and several species of singing birds, are numerous. Turtles are taken upon some of the islands on the coast; and many kinds of excellent fish are caught in the rivers and seas. The rivers swarm with hippopotami, of which numbers are annually taken. It was not until a very recent period that the Portuguese of this settlement paid any attention to the collecting of the teeth of this animal; but finding their sale to be very brisk at Mozambique, they are now sought for with extreme avidity. One of these enormous animals rose by the side of Captain Owen's boat, and taking it in his jaws, at once tore away seven planks from the side and bottom. The forests are inhabited by vast herds of elephants. Captain Owen mentions the arrival of a caravan from the interior to D. bay, consisting of 1,000 native traders, with from 300 to 400 elephants' tusks, and a great quantity of cattle. He was informed that 120 tons of ivory were annually received at Mozambique from this settlement, and that this quantity was in no way considered an extraordinary importation. About 200 m. NW of the Portuguese settlement on D. bay, a town has been founded by emigrant boers from the Cape, called Orichstadt, and a trade in ivory has been established between this town and the bay, but the low swampy country which intervenes between these two points must prove a formidable obstacle to trading movements by land. There is also another boer settlement at Andries-on-Haig, about 60 m. from Lorenzo-Marquez.—The climate of D. bay proved disastrously fatal to the men employed in Captain Owen's surveying expedition. Severe droughts often occur. The natives of D., who are all warriors like the Caffres, are armed with light well-made assagyes. Each adult is provided with a shield cut out from the hide of a bullock, in the brackets of which he carries his spare assagyes, and which thus becomes his quiver. They launch these weapons with great force and dexterity, and can strike a very small object at 80 yds., with sufficient strength for execution. Being continually subject to attacks from warlike tribes, under the general name of Orontatos, or Hollontontes, they are constantly in preparation for war. Among the arts of the Delagonians may be mentioned the erection of huts formed by a kind of wicker process, and rendered very compact. They also make wooden dishes and other utensils, which, considering the rudeness of their implements, are of extraordinary workmanship, and sufficient to prove that they are possessed of a considerable share of taste and ingenuity. The Portuguese lay claim to the entire coast between Cape Guardafui northwards, to the first point of Natal southwards,—a tract of coast of about 2,600 m. A Dutch fort and factory existed in D. bay in the early part of the 18th cent.; but this settlement was destroyed by pirates in 1727. In 1777, the Austrian East India company made a settlement on D. river, but it seems to have been soon after abandoned. Captain Owen attempted to form British settlements near D., in the district called Zembia, and at Mombazo; but he does not appear to have been encouraged in this attempt by our government. A mission was also established at Zembia, and had progressed favourably for two years, when England gave up the infant settlement to Portugal. The Portuguese fort of Lorenzo Marques, on the bay, is 3 H officer in command of the garrison has absolute rule over all the natives within the immediately adjacent district, amongst whom are intermingled a few European settlers who occupy wretched dwellings near the native huts. There is little doubt that the slavetrade is occasionally carried on from this place. Captain Owen states that, under a former commandant, some of the chiefs in the vicinity of D. bay had been persuaded to sell their own subjects for 11 dol. each, paid in merchandise of trifling value! In 1847 it was reported that the boers at Andries-on-Haig were in the habit of sending down natives to the bay for sale. The neighbouring country is divided among different tribes, who are frequently at war with each other, and over whom the Portuguese have little control. The most warlike and troublesome of all these are the Hollotontes. They all practise tattooing, but in a very different manner. Some of them shave off a large portion of the thick woolly covering of their heads. In the 3d vol. of the Geog. Journal, Mr. Cooley warmly advocates the scheme of an exploring expedition to penetrate two or three hundred miles inland from D. bay, and then proceed towards Zambo on the Zambese, and trace that river as far as Tête, from which place a three weeks' voyage would bring the explorers to Quilimane.
DELAIN-JEUNG, a fortress of Northern Hindostan, in Bhotan, 9 m. SW of Tissisudon, and 5 m. E of Dukka-Teung.
DELAMBRE ISLAND, an island of the S. Pacific, in Dampier's archipelago. It is separated from Huiy island by a passage 5 m. wide, though clear for 2 m. only.
